title: Ag Nanocrystals: 1. Effect of Ligands on Plasmonic Properties
publication: The Journal of Physical Chemistry B, 2014
objective: Synthesize and characterize silver nanocrystals (Ag NCs) of varying sizes (2-12 nm) with different surface ligand coatings and investigate how ligand type and alkyl chain length affect plasmonic properties (surface plasmon resonance - SPR)
problem_statement: Understand the effect of coating agent (ligand) on the optical properties of silver nanocrystals, specifically the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak position as a function of nanocrystal size and ligand type

amine_coated.name: Hot Injection Method
steps: Dissolve AgNO3 in DCB with amine coating agent
Rapidly inject into hot dodecanediol solution

thiol_coated.name: Ligand Exchange
thiol_coated.description: Direct hot injection cannot be used for thiol-coated NCs; use ligand exchange from preformed amine-coated NCs
steps: Add thiol coating agent (dodecanethiol or hexadecanethiol)
Stir vigorously for 1 hour at room temperature

key_questions: How does nanocrystal size affect SPR peak position for a given coating agent?
How does coating agent type (amine vs thiol) affect SPR peak position for a given size?
How does alkyl chain length of coating agent affect SPR properties?
What is the efficiency of ligand exchange from amine to thiol?
How do electronic properties (dephasing time) vary with ligand type?

success_metrics: Achieve size distribution coefficient of variation < 10% for each ligand type
SPR peak position predictable from nanocrystal size (R² > 0.95)
Ligand exchange efficiency > 80% as measured by ICP-AES
Nanocrystal morphology confirmed as icosahedral MTPs via TEM
Demonstrate size tuning capability across specified ranges (2.2-12.9 nm)
failure_criteria: SPR peak position inconsistent with literature values (>20 nm deviation)
Evidence of aggregation or phase separation

assumptions: Nanocrystal size is primary determinant of SPR peak position
Ligand coating type and chain length affect optical properties predictably
Room temperature storage maintains nanocrystal stability in toluene
Toluene solvent does not interfere with optical measurements
ICP-AES measurements accurately reflect elemental composition and ligand coverage
Multiplication-twinned particle formation is dominant morphology under stated conditions
known_limitations: Method limited to sizes 2-12 nm; ultra-small (<2 nm) and large (>13 nm) NCs require alternative synthesis
Size control becomes more challenging at extremes of size range
Ligand exchange efficiency may vary with storage time and ligand degradation
SPR measurements sensitive to nanoparticle aggregation; require careful handling
Hot injection method requires specialized equipment and safety precautions (high temperature organic chemistry)
Results specific to noble metal Ag; cannot be directly transferred to Au or other metals without method adaptation
Post-synthesis segregation by size requires additional processing steps and equipment (gel electrophoresis)

alternative_methods_considered.method: Chemical reduction in solution at room temperature
alternative_methods_considered.rationale_rejected: Lower control over nanocrystal size distribution and morphology compared to hot injection
Generates nanocrystals with higher polydispersity and different surface properties
alternative_methods_considered.disadvantage: Less reproducible results; fewer studies on ligand effects

alternative_methods_considered.rationale_rejected: While promising, less well-established for Ag NCs with fine ligand control

alternative_methods_considered.method: Direct surface ligand exchange during synthesis (in-situ)
alternative_methods_considered.rationale_selected_alternative: Tested initially but led to incomplete exchange; post-synthesis approach more reproducible
alternative_methods_considered.outcome: Post-synthesis ligand exchange selected as standard protocol
